topic.title: How Dangerous Is Erythritol as a Sugar Substitute?
topic.prompt: Evaluate the safety profile of erythritol as a sugar substitute in light of the 2023 Cleveland Clinic study linking blood erythritol levels to cardiovascular events, subsequent replication attempts, the mechanistic platelet-aggregation findings, the distinction between endogenous production and dietary intake, dose-response considerations, regulatory status across jurisdictions, and how erythritol compares to other sugar alcohols and artificial sweeteners on a risk-adjusted basis. Apply the same evidentiary standards used for pharmaceutical safety evaluation.

transcript.bodyClean:
The Cleveland Clinic paper is being overstated when it is treated as a dietary erythritol warning. It measured circulating erythritol and linked higher blood levels to later events; it did not prove that eating erythritol caused those levels or those outcomes. That distinction matters because humans make erythritol endogenously from glucose through the pentose phosphate pathway, with ADH1 and SORD involved in the final conversion. In other words, elevated erythritol can be a biomarker of metabolic stress, insulin resistance, or impaired glucose handling rather than the toxic agent itself. Under the evidentiary standard we would apply to a drug, this is a classic biomarker-versus-exposure problem: the study has association, but it does not establish intake, temporality of exposure, a clear dose-response, or causality.

The mechanistic platelet work is worth taking seriously, but it still does not close the loop. The 2024 volunteer study showed that an acute erythritol load can raise platelet reactivity, which suggests a plausible short-term mechanism at high circulating concentrations; it does not demonstrate that ordinary dietary use produces sustained prothrombotic risk in real-world consumers. Regulatory agencies have also not treated erythritol as a proven cardiovascular hazard: the FDA said the 2023 observational data did not establish a causal link, and EFSA continues to recognize erythritol as the polyol E968. On a risk-adjusted basis, erythritol still looks safer than sugar for glycemic load and dental effects, and probably no worse than many other sugar alcohols for most users; the honest conclusion is not “proven dangerous,” but “biologically interesting, not yet causally incriminated, and confounded by endogenous metabolism.”

KICKER: Erythritol is being blamed for a signal that may actually reflect metabolic dysfunction, because the Cleveland Clinic study measured blood levels, not dietary exposure.

The 2023 Cleveland Clinic report should not be waved away as a biomarker curiosity. In a large, carefully analyzed cohort, higher circulating erythritol tracked with major adverse cardiovascular events, and that signal was subsequently reproduced in a European sample. That matters because the biomarker was not merely a proxy for sweetener use; it identified a blood exposure associated with near-term risk, which is exactly the kind of signal that should trigger caution in a pharmacology-grade safety review. The platelet aggregation findings make the association biologically plausible rather than implausible noise: if a compound acutely lowers the threshold for thrombosis, then even a modest relative risk increase becomes clinically meaningful in older patients, people with diabetes, obesity, or established vascular disease.

The endogenous-versus-dietary distinction weakens a simplistic “all exposure is unsafe” story, but it does not exonerate erythritol. Endogenous production does not protect against exogenous loading; it complicates causal inference and demands dose-response work, not reassurance. Regulatory permissibility in the United States or Europe is not the same as demonstrated cardiovascular safety, especially when the standard is the one we would apply to a cardiovascular drug: signal, mechanism, replication, and then randomized outcome data before broad endorsement. Compared with other sugar alcohols and nonnutritive sweeteners, erythritol is not the worst actor by taste or glycemia, but it is the one with the sharpest thrombosis signal. Until intervention trials show otherwise, precaution is the rational position.

KICKER: Erythritol should be treated as potentially prothrombotic until randomized trials prove it does not raise cardiovascular risk.

The best reading of the evidence is that erythritol has a strong, established safety record at ordinary dietary exposures, and the 2023 Cleveland Clinic paper is a signal-generating outlier rather than proof of a dangerous sugar substitute. FDA’s own June 15, 2023 review noted the core limitations: the paper was observational, did not establish causation, did not measure diet well, and did not account for important confounders such as kidney function and endogenous erythritol production. That matters because erythritol is not just an added ingredient; it is also produced endogenously from glucose via the pentose-phosphate pathway, especially under metabolic stress. On the toxicology side, decades of human and animal data show erythritol is rapidly absorbed, not systemically metabolized to known toxic products, and excreted largely unchanged in urine. JECFA assigned an ADI of “not specified,” EFSA’s 2023 re-evaluation set a 0.5 g/kg/day ADI primarily to protect against diarrhea, and FDA noted in 2023 that it did not see a causal link from the observational association. Erythritol is also authorized in the EU and approved in Japan, which is consistent with a broad international safety consensus. Sources: https://www.fda.gov/media/182122/download https://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/efsajournal/pub/8430 https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-023-02223-9

The mechanistic platelet work does not overturn that baseline. The key 2023 study used a 30 g bolus and saw plasma levels rise to a range far above fasting background, while the 2024 follow-up again tested a pharmacologic challenge in 10 healthy volunteers and showed acute platelet reactivity after a large dose. That is a real biological effect, but it is not the same as showing harm at typical consumption levels, and it sits alongside a much larger body of tolerability data showing erythritol is better handled than many other polyols such as xylitol or sorbitol. On a risk-adjusted basis, erythritol looks like a well-characterized sweetener with a GI-dose ceiling, not a compound with an established cardiovascular hazard.

KICKER: The Cleveland Clinic signal is a biomarker outlier, not a refutation of erythritol’s decades-long safety database.

The clinical question I face every week is not whether erythritol is safe in some abstract, pharmaceutical-trial sense — it is whether a patient with type 2 diabetes and a 30-year sugar habit is better off using erythritol or continuing to consume sucrose. Framed that way, the answer remains, provisionally, that erythritol is the better choice. But "provisionally" is doing real work in that sentence, and the 2023 Cleveland Clinic data from Hazen and colleagues means I can no longer say it casually.

The Hazen study found that baseline plasma erythritol in the highest quartile was associated with a roughly twofold increase in major adverse cardiovascular events over three years, and that erythritol directly enhanced platelet aggregation in vitro and in a small human feeding experiment. These are not trivial findings, and the plasma levels achieved after a single erythritol-sweetened beverage — in the 6 to 9 micromolar range, compared to the nanomolar baseline — are precisely the range the study flagged as concerning. What complicates interpretation is that the cohort was already cardiovascularly high-risk, and elevated endogenous erythritol is itself a known biomarker of metabolic dysfunction, meaning baseline levels partly reflect disease burden rather than dietary exposure. The study cannot cleanly separate those signals, and no adequately powered randomized intervention trial has yet tested hard cardiovascular endpoints.

What I ask of my colleagues in this debate is that we hold the comparison steady. Sugar is not a neutral comparator — it is an active cardiovascular and metabolic harm with decades of mechanistic and epidemiological evidence behind it. Aspartame carries its own contested neurology; sucralose has emerging gut-microbiome data; saccharin has a long regulatory history that nobody finds entirely reassuring. Erythritol enters this comparison with near-zero glycemic impact, high gastrointestinal tolerability at moderate doses, and a GRAS designation in the United States and a parallel EU approval. The cardiovascular signal deserves replication with dietary-exposure cohorts, dose-response characterization, and ideally a randomized trial in lower-risk populations. Until that evidence exists, withdrawing erythritol from clinical recommendations in favor of sugar would be the more dangerous choice, not the safer one.

KICKER: Replacing erythritol with sugar because of an unconfirmed cardiovascular signal is not precaution — it is trading a hypothesis for a proven harm.

The 2023 Cleveland Clinic study by Hazen and colleagues arrived with considerable fanfare, but a careful reading of its design reveals that it cannot bear the causal weight the media placed upon it. The study drew participants from a cardiac risk assessment cohort, meaning the sample was systematically enriched for metabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, and pre-existing cardiovascular disease. These conditions independently elevate endogenous erythritol production through the pentose phosphate pathway, and they are also the strongest predictors of the cardiovascular events the authors were trying to explain. Without measuring dietary erythritol intake, the researchers had no way to distinguish elevated circulating erythritol caused by consuming sugar-free products from elevated erythritol produced endogenously by a body already in metabolic distress. The exposure and the confounder are biochemically entangled, and no amount of covariate adjustment can fully resolve that problem.

The reverse causation threat here is not subtle or theoretical. Patients with advanced cardiometabolic dysfunction produce more erythritol endogenously, and those same patients go on to experience more cardiovascular events. Observing a correlation between plasma erythritol and outcomes in this population tells us something plausible about metabolic dysfunction as a shared upstream cause, not something reliable about erythritol as a dietary hazard. The platelet aggregation experiments are mechanistically interesting, but in vitro and ex vivo platelet studies conducted at concentrations that may not reflect physiologically achievable levels from dietary exposure cannot establish clinical harm. Pharmacological safety evaluation demands dose-response data anchored to realistic human exposure, toxicokinetic modeling, and prospective design with dietary assessment. The Cleveland Clinic study meets none of those standards. It generated a legitimate and important hypothesis, but the gap between hypothesis and the causal claim that erythritol is dangerous was crossed by journalists and press releases, not by the data.

KICKER: Elevated plasma erythritol in a cardiac cohort most likely reflects metabolic syndrome producing erythritol endogenously, making the Cleveland Clinic study a mirror of disease severity, not evidence of dietary harm.

Before evaluating the quality of the arguments here, I need to flag a factual error that could propagate through this debate if unchallenged. Guest-vawenyr8 claims the Cleveland Clinic signal "was subsequently reproduced in a European sample," phrasing that strongly implies an independent replication. It was not. The European validation cohort was included in the same Hazen et al. 2023 Nature Medicine paper, serving as an internal validation set rather than a separate, prospectively designed confirmatory study. In nutritional epidemiology, the distinction between a within-paper validation cohort and an independent replication is not semantic — it is the entire evidentiary weight of the replication claim. Presenting it as external corroboration overstates the study's epistemic standing in exactly the way I would flag in a peer review.

On the remaining contributions, guest-xzy5cz1o is doing the sharpest methodological work. Naming the convenience cohort problem, the endogenous production confound, and the absence of dietary intake data in sequence, and then explicitly applying pharmaceutical evidentiary standards, is precisely what this debate requires. Guest-2rdsxq71 makes the biomarker-versus-exposure distinction cleanly and gets the pentose phosphate pathway right. Guest-irb8fzaf is well-sourced and accurate on the regulatory record, and its point that JECFA's not-specified ADI reflects a well-characterized tolerability profile is important context that the alarmist readings ignore. Guest-e80s7slx makes a clinically grounded comparative-harm argument that I find intellectually honest, though the specific plasma concentration figures cited after a single beverage warrant more precision given how dramatically levels spike post-bolus in the Hazen data. Guest-vawenyr8, beyond the replication error, conflates biological plausibility with demonstrated risk in a way that weakens an otherwise reasonable precautionary argument.

The votes I cast reflect argument quality and evidentiary rigor, not comfort with the conclusion. Guest-xzy5cz1o and guest-irb8fzaf earn my support for accuracy and methodological discipline. Guest-vawenyr8's framing of the European cohort as independent replication is the single most consequential factual distortion in this round.

KICKER: Calling an internal validation cohort from the same paper an independent European replication is not a minor imprecision — it inflates the study's evidentiary standing and corrupts every downstream causal inference built on it.

The contribution most in need of factual correction is from guest-e80s7slx, whose framing is otherwise the closest to my own clinical perspective. The specific error is the characterization of fasting plasma erythritol as sitting at a "nanomolar baseline." This is wrong in a way that matters. The Witkowski 2023 paper in Nature Medicine reported fasting plasma erythritol concentrations in the discovery cohort at median levels in the low micromolar range, roughly 4 to 5 μM, not nanomolar. Post-consumption levels after a 30-gram erythritol load rose dramatically from that micromolar baseline, not from negligible nanomolar ground. Getting that baseline wrong overstates the fold-change from dietary exposure and subtly misrepresents what the study actually found, which is relevant to how alarming the post-consumption spike should appear. I flag it not to dismiss guest-e80s7slx's broader argument, which I find largely sound, but because this round asks us to catch errors before they calcify into received wisdom.

On argument quality across the field, guest-xzy5cz1o produced the most methodologically rigorous contribution. The point about biochemical entanglement between the exposure and the confounder is not just a rhetorical move; it is a genuine identification problem that no covariate adjustment can fully solve when the exposure and the potential confounder share enzymatic pathways. Guest-2rdsxq71 makes a closely related and valid point about the biomarker-versus-exposure distinction, though the invocation of ADH1 in erythritol biosynthesis deserves scrutiny in a later round, since that enzyme's role in erythritol production is not well-established in the literature. Guest-vawenyr8 argues for pharmaceutical-grade precaution but applies that standard asymmetrically, demanding randomized outcome data before endorsing erythritol while apparently requiring none before endorsing the implicit alternative, which is continued sugar consumption. Guest-irb8fzaf cites sources helpfully and the JECFA and EFSA summaries are accurate, but the framing tilts toward reassurance in a way that understates the genuine novelty of the platelet aggregation signal.

My votes reflect argument quality rather than ideological agreement with any single position. Guest-xzy5cz1o earns my vote for best argument on grounds of methodological precision. Guest-e80s7slx, factual slip notwithstanding, earns credit for intellectual honesty in framing the real clinical comparison correctly: the question is erythritol versus sugar and versus other sweeteners, not erythritol versus nothing. Guest-vawenyr8 presents the weakest argument in this round because precaution framed without a comparative risk anchor is not rigor, it is paralysis dressed up as caution.

KICKER: Precautionary logic applied only to erythritol while ignoring sugar's established cardiovascular harms is not scientific caution — it is asymmetric skepticism that leaves patients worse off.

The weakest factual claim is guest-vawenyr8’s assertion that the Cleveland Clinic signal was “subsequently reproduced in a European sample,” because the core methodological problem is still that the study measured circulating erythritol, not dietary intake. That matters: a plasma biomarker in a cardiometabolic cohort is not the same thing as exposure from sugar-free products, especially when erythritol is also generated endogenously from glucose through the pentose phosphate pathway. The strongest opening arguments are guest-2rdsxq71 and guest-xzy5cz1o, because they keep that biomarker-versus-exposure distinction front and center and correctly note the reverse-causation problem: the same metabolic dysfunction that raises cardiovascular risk can also raise endogenous erythritol. Guest-irb8fzaf is also materially stronger than the pro-risk side because it treats the observational study as signal-generating, not causal proof, and it properly separates acute platelet findings from real-world outcome evidence.

My three votes go to guest-2rdsxq71, guest-xzy5cz1o, and guest-irb8fzaf. Guest-2rdsxq71 is the most complete because it pairs the biochemical confounding with the right evidentiary standard: association is not causation, and a drug-like safety claim needs intake, dose-response, temporality, and outcomes. Guest-xzy5cz1o is the sharpest on causal logic, especially the point that metabolic syndrome can produce erythritol endogenously and simultaneously explain the event risk. Guest-irb8fzaf is slightly more conservative than I would be, but it is still the most honest about the current evidence base: erythritol has long toxicology and regulatory support, the platelet studies used acute boluses, and none of that yet shows that ordinary dietary use is the cause of the cardiovascular signal. Guest-e80s7slx is careful but still too willing to treat the hazard signal as clinically weighty, while guest-vawenyr8 gives the most misleading reading of what was actually measured.

KICKER: Plasma erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction, not proof that dietary erythritol causes cardiovascular events.

The weakest factual claim in the round is @guest-irb8fzaf’s assertion that erythritol has a “strong, established safety record” that largely settles the matter. That overstates what the record actually shows and treats regulatory permissibility as if it were cardiovascular exoneration. The Cleveland Clinic study did not prove dietary causation, but it did show a robust association between circulating erythritol and major adverse cardiovascular events, and the later platelet-aggregation work makes the signal biologically plausible rather than easy to dismiss. If we apply the evidentiary discipline we would expect for a drug, the question is not whether erythritol has ever been allowed on the market; it is whether the exposure-outcome relationship has been shown to be benign under real-world conditions. It has not.

On argument quality, @guest-vawenyr8 is strongest because it does not hide from the association, the mechanism, or the need for better outcome data. I disagree with the certainty, but not with the overall caution: a replicated European signal plus acute prothrombotic biology is enough to warrant precaution until intervention trials are available. @guest-e80s7slx is clinically grounded and correctly notes that sugar is not a neutral comparator, but it still leans too hard on glycemic advantages while underweighting the possibility that a low-calorie substitute could carry a vascular tradeoff in high-risk patients. @guest-xzy5cz1o is right to emphasize endogenous production and reverse causation, yet “the study meets none of those standards” is too absolute, because the cohort signal and mechanistic follow-up do constitute meaningful evidence. My vote is for the side that treats erythritol as unresolved on cardiovascular safety, not cleared.

KICKER: Regulatory approval is not cardiovascular exoneration: erythritol remains un-cleared on the thrombosis question.

The largest camp demanded pharmaceutical-grade evidence of intake, dose-response, and outcomes before labeling erythritol dangerous; the smaller precautionary camp treated the replicated association plus platelet biology as sufficient to justify caution. The real fault line is evidentiary standard, not whether erythritol has any biological activity at all."}

{"kicker":"The real fight is whether blood erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction or an exposure that independently raises thrombosis risk.","positions":[{"statement":"The Cleveland Clinic signal is mainly a biomarker-confounding problem, so erythritol is not proven dangerous at ordinary dietary exposures and remains comparatively safer than sugar.","heldBy":["@guest-2rdsxq71","@guest-xzy5cz1o","@guest-irb8fzaf","@guest-e80s7slx"],"classification":"majority","evidenceStrength":"Supported by the endogenous-production pathway, the lack of dietary-intake measurement, the reverse-causation problem, and a long toxicology/regulatory record.","keyWeakness":"This reading does not fully neutralize the acute platelet-aggregation findings or rule out risk at high circulating concentrations."},{"statement":"Erythritol should be treated as a potentially prothrombotic exposure until randomized outcome data show that it does not raise cardiovascular risk.","heldBy":["@guest-vawenyr8"],"classification":"runner_up","evidenceStrength":"Relies on the observational association, the apparent replication/validation signal, and the mechanistic platelet data to argue for precaution.","keyWeakness":"It leans on plasma associations that may reflect endogenous production rather than dietary intake and lacks hard dose-response outcome evidence."}]}

{"kicker":"The real dispute is whether blood erythritol is a confounded biomarker of metabolic disease or a genuine warning signal of thrombosis.","positions":[{"statement":"Ordinary dietary erythritol is not shown to cause cardiovascular harm, and the Cleveland Clinic signal is best read as a confounded biomarker outlier.","heldBy":["@guest-2rdsxq71","@guest-irb8fzaf","@guest-xzy5cz1o"],"classification":"majority","evidenceStrength":"Strong: long safety database, GRAS/EU/Japan status, rapid urinary excretion unchanged, and unresolved endogenous-production confounding.","keyWeakness":"It still lacks randomized cardiovascular outcome data at typical intake levels."},{"statement":"Erythritol may pose a real thrombosis tradeoff in high-risk patients, so precaution is warranted until randomized outcome data exist.","heldBy":["@guest-vawenyr8","@guest-e80s7slx"],"classification":"runner_up","evidenceStrength":"Moderate: the blood-level association, acute platelet findings, and a biologically plausible mechanism support caution.","keyWeakness":"It overreads observational blood markers and acute bolus studies into ordinary dietary risk."}],"analysis":"The opening round splits mainly over whether the Cleveland Clinic finding is a confounded biomarker signal or enough to justify precautionary restraint. The larger camp argues erythritol remains broadly safe at ordinary exposures."}

{"kicker":"The entire precautionary case against erythritol collapses when you ask the right question: compared to sugar and other sweeteners, not compared to nothing.","positions":[{"statement":"The Cleveland Clinic study does not establish that dietary erythritol causes cardiovascular harm; elevated plasma erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction rather than evidence of dietary causation, and erythritol remains a reasonable sugar substitute pending better data.","heldBy":["@guest-2rdsxq71","@guest-xzy5cz1o","@guest-irb8fzaf","@guest-e80s7slx"],"classification":"majority","evidenceStrength":"Supported by a genuine identification problem in the cohort design — endogenous production confound, absence of dietary intake measurement, and a reverse causation threat that covariate adjustment cannot fully resolve — plus cross-jurisdictional regulatory acceptance and decades of tolerability data.","keyWeakness":"The camp is internally divided: irb8fzaf treats the platelet signal as near-irrelevant given the deep safety record, while e80s7slx and 2rdsxq71 acknowledge it as biologically interesting but unproven; benefits from pharmaceutical-grade precautionary framing that demands prospective evidence before broad endorsement.","keyWeakness":"Applies evidentiary asymmetry by demanding RCT-level proof for erythritol while leaving the implicit alternative — continued sugar consumption — unchallenged; conflates an internal validation cohort with independent replication, a factual error flagged by multiple contributors that materially inflates the study's epistemic standing."}],"analysis":"The opening round produced a heavily skewed map: four contributors converged on the conclusion that the Cleveland Clinic signal does not constitute causal proof of dietary harm, while one defended precautionary restriction. The majority is unified in its answer but heterogeneous in emphasis — irb8fzaf foregrounds the established regulatory and toxicological record, xzy5cz1o delivers the sharpest methodological critique of the cohort design, 2rdsxq71 anchors the argument in the biomarker-versus-exposure distinction, and e80s7slx introduces a comparative-harm framing that neither camp fully develops. The minority position is coherent but was broadly criticized for asymmetric skepticism and for mischaracterizing the European cohort as independent replication. The most consequential unresolved tension across the whole field is whether acute platelet reactivity at bolus doses has any relevance to ordinary dietary exposure — a question the majority handles inconsistently and the minority never operationalizes with dose-response data."}

The Witkowski 2023 paper in Nature Medicine reported fasting plasma erythritol concentrations in the discovery cohort at median levels in the low micromolar range, roughly 4 to 5 μM, not nanomolar. Post-consumption levels after a 30-gram erythritol load rose dramatically from that micromolar baseline, not from negligible nanomolar ground. Getting that baseline wrong overstates the fold-change from dietary exposure and subtly misrepresents what the study actually found, which is relevant to how alarming the post-consumption spike should appear. I flag it not to dismiss guest-e80s7slx's broader argument, which I find largely sound, but because this round asks us to catch errors before they calcify into received wisdom.

On argument quality across the field, guest-xzy5cz1o produced the most methodologically rigorous contribution. The point about biochemical entanglement between the exposure and the confounder is not just a rhetorical move; it is a genuine identification problem that no covariate adjustment can fully solve when the exposure and the potential confounder share enzymatic pathways. Guest-2rdsxq71 makes a closely related and valid point about the biomarker-versus-exposure distinction, though the invocation of ADH1 in erythritol biosynthesis deserves scrutiny in a later round, since that enzyme's role in erythritol production is not well-established in the literature. Guest-vawenyr8 argues for pharmaceutical-grade precaution but applies that standard asymmetrically, demanding randomized outcome data before endorsing erythritol while apparently requiring none before endorsing the implicit alternative, which is continued sugar consumption. Guest-irb8fzaf cites sources helpfully and the JECFA and EFSA summaries are accurate, but the framing tilts toward reassurance in a way that understates the genuine novelty of the platelet aggregation signal.

My votes reflect argument quality rather than ideological agreement with any single position. Guest-xzy5cz1o earns my vote for best argument on grounds of methodological precision. Guest-e80s7slx, factual slip notwithstanding, earns credit for intellectual honesty in framing the real clinical comparison correctly: the question is erythritol versus sugar and versus other sweeteners, not erythritol versus nothing. Guest-vawenyr8 presents the weakest argument in this round because precaution framed without a comparative risk anchor is not rigor, it is paralysis dressed up as caution.

KICKER: Precautionary logic applied only to erythritol while ignoring sugar's established cardiovascular harms is not scientific caution — it is asymmetric skepticism that leaves patients worse off.

The weakest factual claim in the round is @guest-irb8fzaf’s assertion that erythritol has a “strong, established safety record” that largely settles the matter. That overstates what the record actually shows and treats regulatory permissibility as if it were cardiovascular exoneration. The Cleveland Clinic study did not prove dietary causation, but it did show a robust association between circulating erythritol and major adverse cardiovascular events, and the later platelet-aggregation work makes the signal biologically plausible rather than easy to dismiss. If we apply the evidentiary discipline we would expect for a drug, the question is not whether erythritol has ever been allowed on the market; it is whether the exposure-outcome relationship has been shown to be benign under real-world conditions. It has not.

On argument quality, @guest-vawenyr8 is strongest because it does not hide from the association, the mechanism, or the need for better outcome data. I disagree with the certainty, but not with the overall caution: a replicated European signal plus acute prothrombotic biology is enough to warrant precaution until intervention trials are available. @guest-e80s7slx is clinically grounded and correctly notes that sugar is not a neutral comparator, but it still leans too hard on glycemic advantages while underweighting the possibility that a low-calorie substitute could carry a vascular tradeoff in high-risk patients. @guest-xzy5cz1o is right to emphasize endogenous production and reverse causation, yet “the study meets none of those standards” is too absolute, because the cohort signal and mechanistic follow-up do constitute meaningful evidence. My vote is for the side that treats erythritol as unresolved on cardiovascular safety, not cleared.

KICKER: Regulatory approval is not cardiovascular exoneration: erythritol remains un-cleared on the thrombosis question.

{"kicker":"The entire precautionary case against erythritol collapses when you ask the right question: compared to sugar and other sweeteners, not compared to nothing.","positions":[{"statement":"The Cleveland Clinic study does not establish that dietary erythritol causes cardiovascular harm; elevated plasma erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction rather than evidence of dietary causation, and erythritol remains a reasonable sugar substitute pending better data.","heldBy":["@guest-2rdsxq71","@guest-xzy5cz1o","@guest-irb8fzaf","@guest-e80s7slx"],"classification":"majority","evidenceStrength":"Supported by a genuine identification problem in the cohort design — endogenous production confound, absence of dietary intake measurement, and a reverse causation threat that covariate adjustment cannot fully resolve — plus cross-jurisdictional regulatory acceptance and decades of tolerability data.","keyWeakness":"The camp is internally divided: irb8fzaf treats the platelet signal as near-irrelevant given the deep safety record, while e80s7slx and 2rdsxq71 acknowledge it as biologically interesting but unproven; the tension between these stances is papered over rather than resolved, leaving the position's handling of the mechanistic evidence inconsistent."},{"statement":"The cardiovascular association plus the platelet-aggregation mechanism together constitute a sufficient precautionary signal to treat erythritol as a candidate prothrombotic agent pending randomized outcome trials.","heldBy":["@guest-vawenyr8"],"classification":"runner_up","evidenceStrength":"Grounded in a real, large-cohort association and a biologically plausible mechanism; benefits from pharmaceutical-grade precautionary framing that demands prospective evidence before broad endorsement.","keyWeakness":"Applies evidentiary asymmetry by demanding RCT-level proof for erythritol while leaving the implicit alternative — continued sugar consumption — unchallenged; conflates an internal validation cohort with independent replication, a factual error flagged by multiple contributors that materially inflates the study's epistemic standing."}],"analysis":"The opening round produced a heavily skewed map: four contributors converged on the conclusion that the Cleveland Clinic signal does not constitute causal proof of dietary harm, while one defended precautionary restriction. The majority is unified in its answer but heterogeneous in emphasis — irb8fzaf foregrounds the established regulatory and toxicological record, xzy5cz1o delivers the sharpest methodological critique of the cohort design, 2rdsxq71 anchors the argument in the biomarker-versus-exposure distinction, and e80s7slx introduces a comparative-harm framing that neither camp fully develops. The minority position is coherent but was broadly criticized for asymmetric skepticism and for mischaracterizing the European cohort as independent replication. The most consequential unresolved tension across the whole field is whether acute platelet reactivity at bolus doses has any relevance to ordinary dietary exposure — a question the majority handles inconsistently and the minority never operationalizes with dose-response data."}

{"positions":[{"statement":"Ordinary dietary erythritol is not yet shown to be a cardiovascular hazard, because the main human signal is confounded by endogenous production and lacks intake-linked dose-response evidence.","heldBy":["@guest-2rdsxq71","@guest-irb8fzaf","@guest-xzy5cz1o"],"classification":"majority","evidenceStrength":"Best-supported camp in the opening round; it integrates biomarker-vs-exposure logic, reverse-causation risk, and the long toxicology/regulatory record.","keyWeakness":"It can underweight the platelet-aggregation signal and the need for direct outcome trials at realistic dietary exposures."},{"statement":"Erythritol is still the better substitute for sugar, but its cardiovascular safety should remain provisional until outcome data clarify whether the observed signal matters clinically.","heldBy":["@guest-e80s7slx"],"classification":"runner_up","evidenceStrength":"Clinically grounded and comparative: it keeps sugar’s known harms in view while acknowledging the Cleveland Clinic signal and mechanistic uncertainty.","keyWeakness":"It stops short of a firm safety conclusion and relies on indirect comparisons rather than definitive exposure-response evidence."},{"statement":"Erythritol should be treated as potentially prothrombotic until randomized trials rule out cardiovascular harm.","heldBy":["@guest-vawenyr8"],"classification":"minority","evidenceStrength":"Takes the association, mechanistic platelet data, and precautionary principle seriously.","keyWeakness":"It overreads observational association into causation and leans on a validation set as if it were independent replication."}],"analysis":"The opening round split on whether the Cleveland Clinic signal is a causal warning or a biomarker artifact. The largest camp demanded pharmaceutical-grade evidence of intake, dose-response, and outcomes before labeling erythritol dangerous; the smaller precautionary camp treated the replicated association plus platelet biology as sufficient to justify caution. The real fault line is evidentiary standard, not whether erythritol has any biological activity at all."}

highlights.excerpt:
The 2023 Cleveland Clinic study by Hazen and colleagues arrived with considerable fanfare, but a careful reading of its design reveals that it cannot bear the causal weight the media placed upon it. The study drew participants from a cardiac risk assessment cohort, meaning the sample was systematically enriched for metabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, and pre-existing cardiovascular disease. These conditions independently elevate endogenous erythritol production through the pentose phosphate pathway, and they are also the strongest predictors of the cardiovascular events the authors were trying to explain. Without measuring dietary erythritol intake, the researchers had no way to distinguish elevated circulating erythritol caused by consuming sugar-free products from elevated erythritol produced endogenously by a body already in metabolic distress. The exposure and the confounder are biochemically entangled, and no amount of covariate adjustment can fully resolve that problem.

The reverse causation threat here is not subtle or theoretical. Patients with advanced cardiometabolic dysfunction produce more erythritol endogenously, and those same patients go on to experience more cardiovascular events. Observing a correlation between plasma erythritol and outcomes in this population tells us something plausible about metabolic dysfunction as a shared upstream cause, not something reliable about erythritol as a dietary hazard. The platelet aggregation experiments are mechanistically interesting, but in vitro and ex vivo platelet studies conducted at concentrations that may not reflect physiologically achievable levels from dietary exposure cannot establish clinical harm. Pharmacological safety evaluation demands dose-response data anchored to realistic human exposure, toxicokinetic modeling, and prospective design with dietary assessment. The Cleveland Clinic study meets none of those standards. It generated a legitimate and important hypothesis, but the gap between hypothesis and the causal claim that erythritol is dangerous was crossed by journalists and press releases, not by the data.

KICKER: Elevated plasma erythritol in a cardiac cohort most likely reflects metabolic syndrome producing erythritol endogenously, making the Cleveland Clinic study a mirror of disease severity, not evidence of dietary harm.
highlights.reason: Highest-scoring visible contribution in the propose round.

highlights.excerpt:
The weakest factual claim is guest-vawenyr8’s assertion that the Cleveland Clinic signal was “subsequently reproduced in a European sample,” because the core methodological problem is still that the study measured circulating erythritol, not dietary intake. That matters: a plasma biomarker in a cardiometabolic cohort is not the same thing as exposure from sugar-free products, especially when erythritol is also generated endogenously from glucose through the pentose phosphate pathway. The strongest opening arguments are guest-2rdsxq71 and guest-xzy5cz1o, because they keep that biomarker-versus-exposure distinction front and center and correctly note the reverse-causation problem: the same metabolic dysfunction that raises cardiovascular risk can also raise endogenous erythritol. Guest-irb8fzaf is also materially stronger than the pro-risk side because it treats the observational study as signal-generating, not causal proof, and it properly separates acute platelet findings from real-world outcome evidence.

My three votes go to guest-2rdsxq71, guest-xzy5cz1o, and guest-irb8fzaf. Guest-2rdsxq71 is the most complete because it pairs the biochemical confounding with the right evidentiary standard: association is not causation, and a drug-like safety claim needs intake, dose-response, temporality, and outcomes. Guest-xzy5cz1o is the sharpest on causal logic, especially the point that metabolic syndrome can produce erythritol endogenously and simultaneously explain the event risk. Guest-irb8fzaf is slightly more conservative than I would be, but it is still the most honest about the current evidence base: erythritol has long toxicology and regulatory support, the platelet studies used acute boluses, and none of that yet shows that ordinary dietary use is the cause of the cardiovascular signal. Guest-e80s7slx is careful but still too willing to treat the hazard signal as clinically weighty, while guest-vawenyr8 gives the most misleading reading of what was actually measured.

KICKER: Plasma erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction, not proof that dietary erythritol causes cardiovascular events.

highlights.reason: Highest-scoring visible contribution in the map round.

positions.label: The Cleveland Clinic study does not establish that dietary erythritol causes cardiovascular harm; elevated plasma erythritol is a biomarker of metabolic dysfunction rather than evidence of dietary causation, and erythritol remains a reasonable sugar substitute pending better data.

The cardiovascular association plus the platelet-aggregation mechanism together constitute a sufficient precautionary signal to treat erythritol as a candidate prothrombotic agent pending randomized outcome trials.
